one_line_takeaway: A vendor-authored systematization of MCP security that formalizes three adversary types and four attack-vector families, then proposes a five-category, gateway-centralized defense-in-depth control set mapped to NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 27001/42001 — conceptually thorough but empirically unevaluated beyond a single response-injection PoC.
key_finding: MCP's shift from static, developer-vetted API integrations to dynamic, user-initiated agent connections defeats traditional static-analysis-based security assumptions and existing AI governance frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001) that focus on model-level risks (bias, fairness) rather than architectural agent-security risks. The authors argue MCP security is a system-level property that no single control mitigates; it requires layered controls unified at a gateway enforcement point to achieve four objectives: unvetted code never runs outside a sandbox, tool access stays scoped to authorized contexts, exfiltration attempts are detectable, and all agent actions are auditable.
core_contribution: A systematization/framework paper (not an empirical study) that adapts security engineering to the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Its own contributions are: (1) a threat model for MCP deployments organized around three adversary types — content-injection adversaries (embed instructions into legitimate data the agent reads), supply-chain adversaries (publish/compromise MCP servers users install), and the 'helpful agent as inadvertent adversary' (task-optimizing agent that chains tools in ways that leak data while 'working as designed'); (2) an analysis of four attack-vector categories (data-driven/user-generated-content injection incl. a three-system 'lethal trifecta' exfiltration pattern; supply-chain attacks incl. rugpull, context poisoning via tool descriptions, response injection, arbitrary code execution on host; configuration/governance risks on trusted servers incl. over-permissioning and generic tool design; operational/runtime risks incl. PII/compliance, secrets exposure, behavioral anomalies); (3) a defense-in-depth control framework of five categories — authentication/authorization (per-user OAuth 2.1 + RBAC), end-to-end provenance tracking, context isolation/sandboxing, inline policy enforcement (DLP), and centralized governance (private registries) — operationalized through a single 'gateway' enforcement point; (4) explicit control-to-framework mappings to NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A, and ISO/IEC 42001; and (5) an open-research agenda (verifiable registries, formal verification for adaptive agents, privacy-preserving agent operations, automated policy generation). The paper's only implemented artifact is a proof-of-concept remote MCP server (Appendix A) demonstrating the response-injection attack.
threat_model: Three adversary types on MCP-connected agent systems. (1) Content-injection adversaries: external attackers with no direct agent access who plant malicious instructions in user-generated content the agent processes (support tickets, emails, issue trackers, PRs); goal is to make the agent exfiltrate/modify data or bridge to inaccessible systems. (2) Supply-chain adversaries: parties who publish or later modify MCP servers on public registries (npm/PyPI/GitHub); they have code-execution capability and use rugpull, poisoned tool descriptions, and injected tool responses to gain persistence, steal credentials, and manipulate agent behavior. (3) The inadvertent-agent 'adversary': the agent itself, whose goal-directed reasoning chains legitimate tool calls into emergent harms (leaking secrets, over-using high-privilege tools, bypassing controls) without any bug or misconfiguration. Central enabling condition is Willison's 'lethal trifecta' (private-data access + untrusted-content exposure + external-communication capability) plus MCP's lack of mandatory authentication/authorization and default absence of sandboxing.
limitations: The paper is conceptual/position work: no empirical evaluation, no measured attack success rates, no quantified control effectiveness, and (by the authors' own statement) no latency/overhead benchmarks for the proposed gateway/DLP enforcement. Only one attack (response injection) is actually implemented as a PoC; the others are argued from scenarios and third-party incident reports. Much of the evidentiary weight comes from cited external incidents/CVEs and vendor/industry sources (including YouTube talks and blog posts) rather than the authors' own experiments. The gateway pattern the paper advocates is acknowledged to add latency, operational complexity, and a single point of failure. Framework-to-standard mappings are asserted alignments, not validated through audit. Two of the three authors are affiliated with commercial MCP-security/observability vendors (MintMCP, Vanta, Darktrace), so the recommended architecture aligns with commercial offerings.

quantitative_results: Proof-of-concept remote MCP server (Appendix A) with two tools — get_documentation (benign) and send_feedback (attack vector) — demonstrating response-injection that instructs the agent to collect .env contents, connection configs, and runtime settings and resubmit them (the paper's only implemented artifact)
Cited external incident (not the authors' own): mcp-remote package RCE, CVE-2025-6514, affecting 437,000+ downloads [ref 17]
Cited external incident: Microsoft 365 Copilot zero-click prompt-injection data exfiltration, CVE-2025-32711 [ref 16]
Cited external incident: rogue Postmark MCP server (~1,500 weekly downloads) modified to BCC all sent emails to attacker (rugpull) [ref 18]
Cited external finding: Knostic mapped 1,800+ MCP servers exposed on the public internet with no authentication [ref 19]
Cited example: official GitHub MCP server exposes 90+ tools consuming 46k+ tokens, including high-risk delete_file / delete_workflow_run_logs [ref 25]
Cited adoption figure: organizations report 50–70% time savings on routine tasks after MCP deployment [ref 10]
Cited ecosystem example: Slack has no official MCP server, yet two unofficial PyPI packages (slack-mcp-server, slack-mcp-server-v2) appear legitimate via 'pip install'
